cuisine: Raw Vegetable Reference — Western / Mediterranean origin; available in Singapore at Cold Storage Finest and Marks & Spencer Food
food_subgroup: Cruciferous leafy green, raw. Exceptionally high vitamin K (~817 mcg per 100g = ~681% of daily AI). Also high in vitamin C, calcium, folate, and potassium.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw kale as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw kale serving used as a reference standard is 100g, equivalent to approximately 2–3 large handfuls of loosely packed leaves before any cooking or preparation.

nutrient_source.title: Kale, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 168421)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw kale (FDC ID 168421). All per-100g nutrient values sourced from this entry. Vitamin K value (~817 mcg) is documented in USDA SR Legacy and is consistent with published literature for raw curly and Tuscan kale.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Kale — analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy green vegetables; GI ~15 consistent with published low-GI cruciferous vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Kale does not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~15 is an analogous estimate based on comparable non-starchy cruciferous leafy greens (e.g., cabbage, broccoli) which consistently fall in the GI 10–20 range. Available carbohydrate per 100g = 8.8g total carbohydrate − 3.6g dietary fibre = 5.2g. GL = (15 × 5.2) ÷ 100 = 0.78, rounded to 0.8. This estimate is clinically conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (38 mg/100g), high potassium (491 mg/100g), and high calcium (150 mg/100g) — all DASH diet minerals well-represented
hypertension.guidance: Kale is highly suitable for hypertension management; its low sodium and high potassium-to-sodium ratio support blood pressure reduction, and patients on warfarin should maintain consistent rather than variable kale intake to avoid INR fluctuation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.8 per 100g, and 3.6g dietary fibre per 100g supporting postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kale is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management; its negligible glycemic load and high fibre content slow glucose absorption and support gut microbiome diversity, reducing postprandial glucose spikes from co-consumed carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), high potassium, high vitamin K (~817 mcg/100g) — warfarin interaction is the primary clinical consideration
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kale is cardioprotective due to its antioxidant profile, low saturated fat, and high potassium; patients on warfarin anticoagulation therapy must maintain consistent weekly kale intake rather than avoiding it, as sudden large increases (e.g., daily 150g smoothies) can significantly reduce anticoagulation efficacy by elevating vitamin K intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (120 mg/100g), high folate (141 mcg/100g), and high calcium (150 mg/100g) — particularly beneficial for patients on methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kale is strongly recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate (not warfarin); high folate supports MTX-related folate depletion, high calcium supports bone density in corticosteroid-treated patients, and high vitamin C provides antioxidant support for oxidative stress in RA.

warfarin_interaction.key_factor: Exceptionally high vitamin K content (~817 mcg per 100g = ~681% of daily AI) — highest of any common food
warfarin_interaction.guidance: Patients on warfarin must NOT be advised to avoid kale entirely; instead, advise consistent weekly intake and inform the anticoagulation team of dietary habits, as a sudden daily kale smoothie (150g = ~1,225 mcg vitamin K) without disclosure will measurably reduce anticoagulation efficacy and risk thrombotic events.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kale (Raw) is a cruciferous leafy green vegetable of Mediterranean origin, belonging to the Brassica oleracea species. Per 100g raw: 49 kcal, 4.3g protein, 0.9g fat (0.1g saturated), 8.8g carbohydrate, 3.6g dietary fibre, 38mg sodium, 491mg potassium, 150mg calcium. GI ~15 (very low), GL ~0.8 per 100g. CRITICAL CLINICAL FACT: Kale contains approximately 817 mcg of vitamin K per 100g — the highest of any commonly consumed food, representing ~681% of the daily adequate intake. This is the primary clinical consideration for patients on warfarin anticoagulation therapy. The correct clinical advice is NOT avoidance but CONSISTENT intake — a patient who suddenly begins daily kale smoothies (150g = ~1,225 mcg vitamin K) without informing their anticoagulation team will experience measurable reduction in INR and increased thrombotic risk. Kale is available in Singapore at Cold Storage Finest and Marks & Spencer Food. It is sold as curly kale and Tuscan (lacinato/dinosaur) kale. Texture is firm and slightly bitter raw; softens when massaged with oil or cooked. Suitable as a salad base, smoothie ingredient, or stir-fried leafy green. Strongly recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate: high folate (141 mcg) addresses MTX-induced folate depletion, high calcium (150 mg) supports bone density in corticosteroid-treated patients, and vitamin C (120 mg) provides antioxidant support. Dietary flags: vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168421. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: can I eat kale on warfarin
where to buy kale in Singapore

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168421, an approved primary source with exact match to raw kale. GI is an analogous estimate from comparable non-starchy cruciferous leafy greens, clearly documented. Ontology anchors (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and directly matched. No invented codes. Vitamin K value (~817 mcg) is consistent with published literature and USDA SR Legacy data.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy FDC 168421 — kale is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy — kale is a plant food and naturally contains no vitamin B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy — raw kale contains no vitamin D. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.08g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.34g) values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168421 fatty acid profile. Water_g estimated at 84.0g per USDA SR Legacy (kale raw moisture ~84%). SNOMED CT code not identified for raw kale as a food product; FoodOn URI FOODON_03315119 and FoodEx2 code A00TE provide sufficient ontology anchoring. GI value of ~15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for kale exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Vitamin K content (~817 mcg/100g) is documented in USDA SR Legacy and is the primary clinical consideration for warfarin patients — consistent intake is advised rather than avoidance. This record covers both curly kale (Brassica oleracea var. sabellica) and Tuscan/lacinato kale (Brassica oleracea var. palmifolia); nutrient profiles are sufficiently similar for a single reference record.
